Amlodipine 10mg + Atorvastatin 40mg: A fixed-dose combination of Amlodipine 10mg (calcium channel blocker) and Atorvastatin 40mg (high-intensity statin). Amlodipine blocks vascular L-type calcium channels, causing arterial vasodilation and reducing peripheral resistance to lower high blood pressure. Atorvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, reducing LDL cholesterol by 50–60%. Co-prescribed for patients with concurrent hypertension and high cholesterol to simplify therapy and improve cardiovascular outcomes.
Amlodipine 2.5mg: A low starting dose of Amlodipine, a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker. Blocks L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation and lowering blood pressure. The 2.5mg dose is used to initiate therapy in elderly, frail, or sensitive patients with mild hypertension to minimize the risk of hypotension and peripheral edema. Titrated upward to 5mg or 10mg based on blood pressure response and tolerability.
Amlodipine 5mg: Standard therapeutic dose of Amlodipine, a first-line calcium channel blocker for hypertension per JNC 8 guidelines. Inhibits vascular smooth muscle calcium entry, causing sustained arterial vasodilation and blood pressure reduction. Effective across all adult age groups including elderly patients with isolated systolic hypertension. Once-daily dosing ensures high adherence. Does not significantly affect serum electrolytes or Na/K balance.
Amlodipine 5mg + Atorvastatin 20mg: Fixed-dose combination of Amlodipine 5mg (calcium channel blocker) and Atorvastatin 20mg (moderate-intensity statin). Addresses two major cardiovascular risk factors simultaneously: high blood pressure and elevated LDL cholesterol. Amlodipine dilates peripheral arteries to reduce blood pressure; Atorvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, lowering LDL by 38–46%. Reduces pill burden and improves treatment adherence in patients with hypertension and dyslipidemia.
Aspirin 100mg: Low-dose antiplatelet agent that irreversibly acetylates and inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) in platelets, blocking thromboxane A2 synthesis and reducing platelet aggregation. Prescribed at 100mg for secondary prevention of myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular death in patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). Not recommended for primary prevention in low-to-moderate risk individuals per current ACC/AHA 2019 guidelines.
Aspirin 75mg: Low-dose antiplatelet used for long-term cardiovascular risk reduction. Irreversibly inhibits platelet COX-1, reducing thromboxane A2 production and arterial thrombosis risk. The 75mg dose delivers equivalent antiplatelet effect to 100mg with marginally lower gastrointestinal bleeding risk. Used in elderly patients and those with established cardiovascular disease for secondary prevention. Often co-prescribed with Omeprazole to reduce GI complications.
Atorvastatin 10mg: Low-intensity statin for patients with mild hyperlipidemia or those initiating statin therapy who are statin-sensitive. Inhibits hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, reducing LDL cholesterol by approximately 30–37%. Suitable for younger patients or those at lower cardiovascular risk. Best taken in the evening to align with peak hepatic cholesterol synthesis. Well-tolerated at this dose with minimal risk of myopathy.
Atorvastatin 20mg: Moderate-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 38–46% through HMG-CoA reductase inhibition. First-line lipid-lowering therapy per ACC/AHA guidelines for patients with elevated cardiovascular risk and high cholesterol. Also provides plaque-stabilizing and anti-inflammatory effects independent of lipid lowering. Once-daily dosing at any time. Liver function should be checked if symptoms of hepatotoxicity develop.
Atorvastatin 40mg: High-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 50–60%. Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to markedly reduce hepatic cholesterol synthesis and increase LDL receptor expression. Recommended for patients with established ASCVD, familial hypercholesterolemia, or very high cardiovascular risk per ACC/AHA and NICE guidelines. Standard of care for secondary cardiovascular prevention. Monitor for myalgia, elevated creatine kinase, and hepatic transaminases at initiation.
Calcium + Vitamin D3 500mg/400IU: Combined bone health supplement providing Calcium 500mg and Vitamin D3 400IU. Calcium supports bone mineral density, nerve signal transmission, and muscle contraction. Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) is hydroxylated to active calcitriol, which enhances intestinal calcium absorption and promotes renal calcium reabsorption. Prescribed for prevention of osteoporosis in elderly patients, postmenopausal women, and individuals with low dietary calcium intake or limited sun exposure.
Calcium Carbonate 500mg: Oral calcium supplement containing 40% elemental calcium. Used to correct hypocalcemia, prevent osteoporosis, and supplement dietary calcium deficiency. Requires adequate gastric acid for optimal absorption; best taken with meals. Also used as a phosphate binder in chronic kidney disease. Adjunct therapy in patients on long-term corticosteroids or proton pump inhibitors that reduce calcium absorption.
Cholestyramine 4g: Bile acid sequestrant that binds bile acids in the intestinal lumen, preventing enterohepatic recirculation and forcing the liver to convert more cholesterol into new bile acids, reducing LDL cholesterol by 15–30%. Used for hypercholesterolemia in statin-intolerant patients. Not systemically absorbed — safe in pregnancy. Administer other drugs 1 hour before or 4–6 hours after Cholestyramine to avoid absorption interference.
Digoxin 0.125mg: Cardiac glycoside that inhibits Na+/K+-ATPase in myocardial cells, increasing intracellular calcium and enhancing contractility. Also slows AV nodal conduction to control ventricular rate in atrial fibrillation. Low dose of 0.125mg used in elderly patients due to narrow therapeutic index (target: 0.5–0.9 ng/mL). Regular serum digoxin levels, renal function, and potassium monitoring required to avoid toxicity.
Enalapril 10mg: ACE inhibitor that blocks angiotensin-converting enzyme, preventing angiotensin II formation. Reduces peripheral vascular resistance, aldosterone secretion, and sodium retention to lower blood pressure. Used for hypertension and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. Dry cough in 5–20% of patients. Contraindicated in bilateral renal artery stenosis and pregnancy.
Ezetimibe 10mg: Cholesterol absorption inhibitor blocking the intestinal NPC1L1 transporter, reducing dietary cholesterol absorption by 55%. Reduces LDL by 18–25% as monotherapy. Used in statin-intolerant patients or as adjunct to statins for additional LDL lowering. Minimal systemic absorption ensures favorable safety profile. Once-daily dosing at any time.
Ezetimibe 10mg + Simvastatin 20mg: Fixed-dose combination of Ezetimibe 10mg and Simvastatin 20mg. Dual mechanism: Simvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase; Ezetimibe blocks intestinal cholesterol absorption. Combined LDL reduction of 45–55%, superior to monotherapy. Prescribed for high cholesterol requiring greater LDL lowering or statin intolerance at higher doses.
Fludrocortisone 0.1mg: Synthetic mineralocorticoid promoting renal sodium and water reabsorption to raise blood pressure. First-line treatment for orthostatic hypotension and adrenal insufficiency. Requires monitoring for edema, hypokalemia, and supine hypertension with long-term use.
Furosemide 40mg: Potent loop diuretic inhibiting the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in the loop of Henle, producing rapid sodium, potassium, and water excretion. Used for resistant hypertension and fluid overload. Strictly contraindicated in hypotension. Requires regular electrolyte monitoring due to hypokalemia risk.
Hydrochlorothiazide 25mg: Thiazide diuretic and first-line antihypertensive per JNC 8. Inhibits Na-Cl cotransporter in the distal convoluted tubule, reducing sodium and water reabsorption to lower blood pressure. Preferred when Na/K ratio is elevated. Monitor for hypokalemia, hyperuricemia, and hyperglycemia.
Indapamide 1.5mg: Thiazide-like diuretic with vasodilatory and mild diuretic properties. At 1.5mg, acts primarily as a vasodilator, reducing peripheral resistance with fewer metabolic side effects than conventional thiazides. Metabolically neutral — preferred over HCTZ in diabetic and dyslipidemic hypertensive patients.
Iron + Folic Acid 60mg/5mg: Combined haematinic providing ferrous iron 60mg and folic acid 5mg. Iron replenishes stores and supports hemoglobin synthesis. Folic acid is essential for DNA synthesis and red blood cell maturation. Prescribed for iron-deficiency and megaloblastic anemia, and high-risk pregnancy to prevent neural tube defects.
Levothyroxine 25mcg: Low starting dose of synthetic T4 for hypothyroidism. Used in elderly patients or those with cardiac disease to minimize risk of arrhythmia from rapid thyroid hormone increase. Peripherally converted to active T3 to regulate metabolism and cardiovascular function. Dose titrated every 6–8 weeks based on TSH levels.
Levothyroxine 50mcg: Standard synthetic T4 replacement for hypothyroidism in younger adults. Restores euthyroid state, normalizing TSH and resolving fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance. Taken on empty stomach 30–60 minutes before breakfast for optimal absorption. Annual TSH monitoring required once stable.
Lisinopril 10mg + Atorvastatin 20mg: Combination of ACE inhibitor Lisinopril 10mg and moderate statin Atorvastatin 20mg. Lisinopril blocks ACE to reduce vasoconstriction and lower blood pressure with renal protection. Atorvastatin reduces LDL by 38–46%. Together, they significantly reduce 10-year cardiovascular event risk in patients with both high BP and high cholesterol.
Lisinopril 20mg + Rosuvastatin 20mg: Combination of ACE inhibitor Lisinopril 20mg and high-intensity statin Rosuvastatin 20mg. Lisinopril 20mg provides robust BP reduction with nephroprotection in diabetic or CKD patients. Rosuvastatin 20mg reduces LDL by 52–55%. Maximally reduces cardiovascular mortality in high-risk hypertensive patients with hyperlipidemia.
Losartan 50mg: ARB that selectively blocks AT1 receptors, preventing angiotensin II-mediated vasoconstriction and aldosterone release. Preferred over ACE inhibitors in female patients due to no cough risk. Provides renoprotection in diabetic nephropathy. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Must not be combined with ACE inhibitors.
Losartan 50mg + Rosuvastatin 10mg: Combination of ARB Losartan 50mg and moderate statin Rosuvastatin 10mg for hypertension with high cholesterol. Losartan lowers BP via AT1 blockade; Rosuvastatin reduces LDL by 45–50% and raises HDL by 8–10%. Reduces pill burden while addressing two independent cardiovascular risk factors.
Metformin 500mg: First-line biguanide for type 2 diabetes. Inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis and improves peripheral insulin sensitivity without hypoglycemia. Starting dose of 500mg with meals minimizes GI side effects. UKPDS trial demonstrated significant reduction in diabetes complications and cardiovascular mortality. Contraindicated in eGFR <30 mL/min due to lactic acidosis risk.
Metoprolol 50mg + Atorvastatin 10mg: Combination of beta-1 blocker Metoprolol 50mg and low-intensity statin Atorvastatin 10mg. Metoprolol reduces heart rate and cardiac output to lower BP, beneficial in angina or post-MI. Atorvastatin provides mild LDL reduction. Co-prescribed for high BP with mild dyslipidemia requiring both heart rate control and lipid lowering.
Metoprolol Succinate 50mg: Extended-release cardioselective beta-1 blocker providing 24-hour sustained BP control. Reduces heart rate, contractility, and cardiac output. Second- or third-line antihypertensive per JNC 8. First-line for HFrEF, angina, and post-MI. Monitor for bradycardia, fatigue, and bronchoconstriction in asthma patients.
Midodrine 5mg: FDA-approved alpha-1 agonist for orthostatic hypotension. Prodrug converted to desglymidodrine, constricting peripheral arterioles to raise standing BP by 10–30 mmHg. Must not be taken within 4 hours of bedtime to avoid supine hypertension. First-line pharmacological treatment for symptomatic low BP.
Normal Saline Oral Solution: Isotonic oral sodium chloride solution for fluid and electrolyte replacement in mild dehydration and hypotension. Raises intravascular sodium and water to restore circulating volume and blood pressure. First-line non-pharmacological intervention for mild hypotension from hypovolemia before escalating to vasopressors.
ORS + Zinc 20mg: WHO-recommended oral rehydration solution combined with Zinc 20mg. ORS restores fluid and electrolyte balance via glucose-sodium co-transport. Zinc enhances gut mucosal recovery, shortens diarrheal illness, and boosts innate immunity. For electrolyte correction and dehydration associated with elevated Na/K ratio.
Omega-3 Fatty Acids 1000mg: Marine-derived EPA + DHA at 1000mg for dyslipidemia. Reduces hepatic VLDL synthesis, increases triglyceride clearance, and provides anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective benefits. First-line non-statin therapy for mild dyslipidemia in younger adults. Well-tolerated; main side effect is fishy aftertaste.
Omeprazole 20mg: PPI irreversibly inhibiting gastric H+/K+-ATPase, reducing acid secretion by over 90%. Prescribed for GERD, peptic ulcers, H. pylori eradication, and gastroprotection in patients on NSAIDs or aspirin. Often co-prescribed with cardiovascular medications to prevent GI bleeding. Taken 30 minutes before a meal.
Oral Rehydration Salts: WHO-standard ORS providing sodium, potassium, chloride, citrate, and glucose for fluid and electrolyte restoration via intestinal glucose-sodium co-transport. First-line treatment for mild-to-moderate dehydration and hypotension. Reduces IV rehydration need. Safe for all ages.
Oral Rehydration Salts + NaCl Tabs 600mg: Standard ORS combined with NaCl 600mg tablets for significant sodium depletion and hypotension. Additional sodium raises intravascular concentration to correct serum sodium deficit and restore BP. Used before escalation to IV saline. Monitor serum sodium to avoid overly rapid correction.
Paracetamol 500mg: Analgesic and antipyretic inhibiting central prostaglandin synthesis via COX modulation. Favorable cardiovascular profile — no effect on platelets, BP, or renal prostaglandins unlike NSAIDs. Preferred analgesic for patients on antihypertensives or antiplatelets. Maximum 4g/day in healthy adults; 2g/day in liver disease or alcohol use.
Potassium Chloride 600mg: Oral potassium supplement (8 mEq/tablet) for hypokalemia correction when Na/K ratio falls below 10. Preferred formulation per AAFP, especially with concurrent metabolic alkalosis or diuretic-induced potassium loss. Contraindicated in hyperkalemia and eGFR <30 mL/min. Monitor serum potassium every 2–4 hours during active repletion.
Ramipril 2.5mg: Low starting dose of ACE inhibitor Ramipril for hypertension or heart failure. Blocks ACE to reduce angiotensin II and lower vasoconstriction. Used in elderly or renally impaired patients prone to first-dose hypotension. HOPE trial demonstrated significant cardiovascular mortality reduction. Titrated to 5–10mg maintenance dose.
Ramipril 5mg + Atorvastatin 10mg: Combination of ACE inhibitor Ramipril 5mg and low-intensity statin Atorvastatin 10mg for mild hypertension with mild dyslipidemia. Ramipril provides BP control with nephroprotection; Atorvastatin lowers LDL by 30–37%. Additive cardiovascular risk reduction for patients with diabetes or early CKD and mild lipid elevation.
Rosuvastatin 10mg: Moderate statin with high hepatic selectivity reducing LDL by 45–50% and raising HDL by 8–10%. Minimal systemic exposure minimizes myopathy risk. Prescribed for moderate cardiovascular risk. Long 19-hour half-life allows once-daily dosing at any time. Lower myopathy risk than equivalent doses of simvastatin.
Rosuvastatin 20mg: High-intensity statin reducing LDL by 52–55%. Supported by JUPITER and SATURN trial evidence for primary and secondary cardiovascular prevention. Preferred for high-risk patients with established ASCVD or markedly elevated LDL. Dose adjustment required in Asian patients and severe renal impairment.
Rosuvastatin 20mg + Fenofibrate 145mg: Combination of Rosuvastatin 20mg and Fenofibrate 145mg for mixed dyslipidemia. Rosuvastatin reduces LDL; Fenofibrate activates PPAR-alpha to reduce triglycerides by 30–50% and raise HDL by 10–20%. Fenofibrate is the preferred fibrate with statins due to lower myopathy risk compared to gemfibrozil.
Rosuvastatin 40mg: Very high-intensity statin for maximum LDL reduction of 55–65% in familial hypercholesterolemia or very high-risk ASCVD patients. Used when lower doses fail to meet LDL targets. Often combined with Ezetimibe for added efficacy. Monitor liver enzymes and CK at initiation. Capped at 20mg in Asian populations.
Simvastatin 10mg: Low-to-moderate statin reducing LDL by 27–35%. Suitable for mild hypercholesterolemia or statin-sensitive patients. FDA 2011 safety alert: dose must not exceed 10mg with amlodipine, amiodarone, or ranolazine due to myopathy risk at higher doses. Evening dosing preferred.
Simvastatin 20mg: Moderate statin reducing LDL by 35–42%. For moderate cardiovascular risk patients with hypercholesterolemia or mixed dyslipidemia. Evening dosing aligns with peak cholesterol synthesis. FDA advises against 80mg dose; 20mg is a safe and effective alternative. Monitor for myalgia periodically.
Spironolactone 25mg: Potassium-sparing diuretic and aldosterone antagonist blocking distal tubule aldosterone receptors to reduce sodium retention without potassium loss. Third-line antihypertensive for resistant hypertension when first-line agents are insufficient. Also indicated for HFrEF (RALES trial) and primary hyperaldosteronism. Contraindicated in hypotension, hyperkalemia, and eGFR <30 mL/min.
Vitamin D3 60000IU weekly: High-dose weekly pulse of cholecalciferol for correction of moderate-to-severe vitamin D deficiency (25-OH-D <20 ng/mL). Hydroxylated to active calcitriol, enhancing calcium absorption, bone mineralization, immune regulation, and cardiovascular protection. Prescribed for 8–12 weeks then maintenance dose. Monitor serum calcium and 25-OH-D after loading course.
